---
suppress-bibliography: true
bibliography: references.bib
---

# Introduction

The globally increasing incidence of type 2 diabetes (T2D) suggests that current prevention strategies remain inadequate to slow progression from prediabetes to T2D [@saeedi2019]. Current risk stratification in prediabetes relies on glycemic states such as impaired fasting glucose and impaired glucose tolerance, which arise from a combination of insulin resistance and declining beta-cell function [@echouffo-tcheugui2021]. While glucose patterns during an oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) help characterize glycemic states, they provide limited insights into broader metabolic heterogeneity. While heterogeneity in T2D is well-established [@ahlqvist2018; @misra2023; @li2024], emerging evidence suggests that this heterogeneity is already present among individuals with prediabetes, highlighting an opportunity for more targeted prevention among individuals at high risk of T2D before diabetes onset [@wagner2026; @onthoni2025; @wagner2021].

Beyond glucose, the OGTT also has the potential to capture postprandial hormonal responses, which may represent another key dimension of metabolic heterogeneity in prediabetes. The incretin effect, which is the elevated insulin response to oral versus intravenous glucose [@holst2019], is primarily driven by glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) and glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide (GIP), which have an important impact on the postprandial insulin response and glucose regulation [@nauck1986]. GLP-1 enhances insulin secretion and suppresses glucagon, while GIP primarily stimulates insulin secretion. This makes GLP-1 a key regulator of postprandial glucose regulation, demonstrated by the success of GLP-1-based therapies in treating type 2 diabetes and obesity [@holst2024]. Contrary to the glucose-lowering mechanisms of GLP-1 and GIP, glucagon increases glucose levels by stimulating glycogenolysis in the liver, making its suppression during glucose intake essential for normal glucose regulation [@ahrén2015]. However, the interplay between incretin secretion and glucagon suppression among individuals with impaired glucose metabolism is complex, underscored by conflicting findings in the literature. Incretin secretion has been found to be decreased [@laakso2008; @muscelli2006; @toft-nielsen2001], normal [@vollmer2008; @ahren1997] or even increased [@chong2022; @færch2008] among individuals with impaired glucose metabolism. Similarly, glucagon suppression appears to vary with insulin sensitivity, as individuals with prediabetes and screen-detected T2D exhibit impaired early but relatively normal late glucagon suppression, whereas individuals with higher insulin sensitivity show higher early but lower late glucagon suppression [@færch2016; @wagner2017].

In addition to postprandial glycemic and hormonal responses, body fat distribution represents another important dimension of metabolic heterogeneity. In particular, visceral adipose tissue (VAT) and subcutaneous adipose tissue (SAT) are closely linked to both insulin resistance and beta-cell function [@kahn2001], and fat distribution already differs among glycemic subtypes of individuals with prediabetes [@borel2015]. This is further supported by recent phenotyping studies identifying subgroups with distinct characteristics, including diverse patterns of body fat distribution [@wagner2026; @stroebel2025]. However, while both hormonal responses and body fat distribution are recognized as important contributors to metabolic risk, these dimensions are typically studied separately. Consequently, it remains unclear how the interplay between OGTT-derived measures of glucose and insulin together with incretins and glucagon relates to different patterns of body fat distribution among individuals with prediabetes.

To assess this, the aim of this study was to identify metabolic subgroups among individuals at high risk of T2D using k-means clustering of OGTT-derived glucose and hormonal responses, and to quantify the associations between these subgroups and measures of body fat distribution.
